    17. Why The Website You're Building Is Already Obsolete
</h2>

  <!-- Attachment Blocks -->
<!-- DEBUG: attachments.count=2 -->

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=18142891, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-18142891">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>Does your website have an "about" page?</p><p>Does it have social media icons so that people can connect with you throughout the internet?</p><p>Do you have a drop down menu or a header with different important page links?</p><p>Do you have a sign-up button for your newsletter? </p><p>Then your website is nothing more than a brochure. And no-one has time for a brochure without a succinct message these days.</p><p>Maybe that worked 10 years ago but it doesn't now.</p><p>If you send your valuable fans to the kind of website I just described then you hate money. You might as well open your wallet and empty it into a trash can. </p><p>What you need is a landing page.</p><p>A landing page is a "website" that only has one goal. And when people land on it... they know exactly what they need to do. They don't have to think about anything beyond a "yes" or "no". Nothing is confusing.</p><p>Confusion never leads to getting more fans. </p><p>Confusion never leads to sales.</p><p><strong>Confusion only leads to "Good-bye, I'm going back to my IG now..."</strong></p><p>No, you don't need an about page. The whole internet is your "about page". No one finds out more about you by reading your about page... They don't believe whatever you write there anyways (in the third person no doubt). They know it's all hype.</p><p>When people wanna know more "about" you, they Google you. Or they read your captions on IG. Or your posts on Facebook. Or they sign up to your mailing list.</p><p>If your goal is to announce your tour. Make your landing page the dates and put a link to buy tickets. Don't distract them with anything else. Now is not the time to tell them about your merch!</p><p>If your goal is to get their email... Then only put an email opt-in form on your page. Nothing else. You'll follow up with extra information about your tour and your merch in your "welcome email sequence". You can send them to those specific pages later... As you communicate with them over the weeks.</p><p><strong>You should be a storytelling machine. And your landing page should drop them right in the middle of the action. Don’t give them a million options. This isn’t a choose your own adventure... No. You’re Quentin Tarantino and you’re taking them on a journey. They don’t get options.</strong></p><p>Just because Squarespace" or "Wix" put it in the template doesn't mean you have to use it! Don't go through the motions. Don't copy what other people do. Study your own behavior. What do YOU do when you navigate the internet? Design the online experience that would drive your psychographic to tears of joy.</p><p>The "customer's journey" is all that matters.</p><p>And wherever that journey starts... it ultimately lands on your "website".</p><p><em>So make sure that when they get there they know exactly what to do.</em></p><p><em>Here's a photographer website that forces you to do what they want you to do:</em></p><p><a href="https://nukemsupply.com/" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" style="color: rgb(38, 38, 38); background-color: rgb(250, 250, 250);">HARIS NUKEM</a></p><p></p>
</div>
